skill.name: web-component-design
skill.description: Design reusable React, Vue, and Svelte UI components with composition patterns, styling guidance, accessibility practices, and component API conventions.
skill.summary: Build reusable frontend components and design-system patterns across React, Vue, and Svelte.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, credential, weak crypto, network, and dynamic import patterns, but review found these are Markdown and TypeScript examples for frontend component design. No prompt injection, credential access, shell execution, or malicious exfiltration intent was found; only low-risk illustrative fetch and dynamic import snippets remain as publishable cautions.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Fetch API Example
low_findings.description: The fetch call is inside a documented React render-props example and uses a caller-provided URL. It is not executed by the skill, but copied code should restrict URLs when used with untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is explicit and located in instructional code. Confidence is high that it is benign because there is no credential handling or external hardcoded endpoint.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to public framework and component documentation. These URLs are normal resource references and do not send user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are Markdown resource links only. No executable network request or data transfer is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences and TypeScript template literals. They document frontend component examples and are not executable shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are Markdown fences or frontend syntax. No shell interpreter, command API, or user-controlled command string appears in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Keyboard and React Key Usage Misidentified as Sensitive Keys
dangerous_patterns.description: The sensitive key findings refer to KeyboardEvent key checks and React list keys in accessibility examples. No certificates, private keys, tokens, or credentials were found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is keyboard handling and React rendering. The broader keyword search found no private key blocks or credential material.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Import Examples Are Framework Syntax
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import hits are a Svelte type reference and a Next.js code-splitting example. They do not load remote code or evaluate untrusted strings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both imports use static framework module paths or type references. There is no dynamic URL, remote module source, or user input in the import target.

dangerous_patterns.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Heuristics Not Confirmed
dangerous_patterns.description: The reported weak cryptography and reconnaissance lines map to CSS class names, component props, keyboard handling, and type syntax. No cryptographic API use or system reconnaissance behavior was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative lines are ordinary frontend examples. No imports or calls for MD5, SHA1, crypto libraries, host enumeration, or file-system inspection were found.

content.value_statement: Frontend teams need components that stay consistent as products grow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through component APIs, composition patterns, styling choices, and accessibility checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains React compound components, render props, context, and polymorphic component patterns.
Compares Tailwind CSS, CSS Modules, styled-components, Emotion, and Vanilla Extract use cases.
Provides accessible modal, dropdown, combobox, form, and focus-management patterns.
Guides component API design with typed props, variants, defaults, and composition support.
Covers Vue composables, slots, provide and inject, and Svelte 5 rune-based component examples.
Helps refactor legacy UI components toward reusable design-system conventions.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete production component library.
Generated code still needs project-specific testing, visual review, and accessibility validation.
It does not replace framework documentation for version-specific API changes.
It does not audit package dependencies or runtime bundle output by itself.

use_cases.title: Create a Design-System Component
use_cases.description: Define variants, props, accessibility behavior, and styling rules for a reusable button, dialog, tab, or form control.

use_cases.title: Standardize Component APIs
use_cases.description: Review existing components and align naming, defaults, composition, and override patterns across a product codebase.

use_cases.title: Compare Framework Patterns
use_cases.description: Translate a UI pattern between React, Vue, and Svelte while preserving state flow and accessibility behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Basic Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-component-design skill to design a reusable [component name] for [framework]. Include props, variants, accessibility behavior, and styling guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor an Existing Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-component-design skill to review this [framework] component. Suggest a cleaner component API, composition model, and styling approach.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Design-System Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-component-design skill to design a [pattern] component family. Include root, subcomponents, state ownership, keyboard support, and usage examples.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate Cross-Framework Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the web-component-design skill to compare React, Vue, and Svelte implementations for [component pattern]. Recommend one architecture and explain tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Design a reusable React tabs component for a design system.
output_examples.output: A component plan with root and child parts, controlled and uncontrolled state options, ARIA roles, keyboard behavior, and styling variants.

output_examples.input: Choose a styling approach for a multi-brand component library.
output_examples.output: A comparison of CSS Modules, utility classes, CSS-in-JS, and zero-runtime styling, with a recommended approach for theme tokens.

output_examples.input: Improve accessibility for a custom dropdown menu.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering focus handling, roles, keyboard navigation, labels, disabled states, and screen reader behavior.

best_practices: Start with semantic HTML and accessibility behavior before adding visual variants.
Keep component APIs small, typed, and consistent across the design system.
Test examples with keyboard navigation, responsive layouts, and realistic content.
anti_patterns: Do not add many one-off props when composition would express the same behavior more clearly.
Do not copy fetch examples into production without validating allowed URLs and data handling.
Do not treat visual styling as complete until accessibility and interaction states are checked.

faq.question: Does this skill create production-ready components?
faq.answer: It provides strong implementation patterns and examples. You still need project tests, accessibility checks, and visual review.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers React, Vue, and Svelte, with extra guidance for shared design-system ideas.

faq.question: Can it help choose a CSS strategy?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares utility classes, CSS Modules, CSS-in-JS, and zero-runtime styling approaches.

faq.question: Does it include accessibility guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for dialogs, dropdowns, comboboxes, forms, focus traps, and contrast checks.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found no malicious intent, credential access, shell execution, or prompt injection.

faq.question: Why is the risk level low instead of safe?
faq.answer: The files include instructional fetch and dynamic import examples. They are benign, but copied code should still be reviewed.
